site.name: Bitfield
site.description: World's fastest plug-in runtime and database for founders who can't afford to slow down when plans change.

social.title: Bitfield Docs
social.description: Build fast today and keep your 100th feature as fast as your first with Bitfield.
social.imageAlt: World's fastest plug-in runtime and database for founders who can't afford to slow down when plans change. Bitfield.

pages.title: Bitfield Docs
pages.description: Build fast today and keep your 100th feature as fast as your first with Bitfield.
pages.summary: The public entry point and path chooser for understanding Bitfield, trying it, building a first screen, checking proof, or managing account access.

pages.title: Quickstart
pages.description: Get a key, add Runtime Kit, read data, and send a request.
pages.summary: A first-result tutorial for account access, device activation, Runtime Kit imports, reading prepared data, sending a target request, and handling first failures.

pages.title: Get your key
pages.description: How keys, checkout, account access, and the first device activation fit together.
pages.summary: Explains the account portal path for account creation, trial or plan access, email confirmation, key access, first device activation, recovery, and next steps.

pages.title: Manifesto
pages.description: Why Bitfield exists and what future software is allowed to become.
pages.summary: Jibran Akhtar's founder manifesto for Bitfield: future software, growing ideas, curiosity, and why Bitfield exists.

pages.title: What is Bitfield?
pages.description: A customer-facing explanation of Bitfield as a database and runtime.
pages.summary: Explains Bitfield without assuming the reader already knows database or runtime vocabulary.

pages.title: Database and runtime
pages.description: What Bitfield stores, what it runs, and why those two pieces belong together.
pages.summary: Defines the public database and runtime model.

pages.title: Storage shape
pages.description: How Bitfield keeps local durable data fast without publishing private implementation details.
pages.summary: Explains local durable truth, memory-mapped local files, content identity, and the current view.

pages.title: Content-addressed storage
pages.description: What content addresses are and why they matter for speed, integrity, and reuse.
pages.summary: Defines content addresses and states what the public docs do not expose.

pages.title: Slots
pages.description: What a Bitfield slot is and why slots keep features from tangling together.
pages.summary: Explains slots as named runnable doors that features can call without importing each other.

pages.title: Active devices
pages.description: What counts as an active device and how revoking or replacing a device works.
pages.summary: Defines active devices for pricing, keys, and account management.

pages.title: JavaScript Runtime Kit
pages.description: Understand the public Runtime Kit surface, package boundary, and where React fits.
pages.summary: Maps the public Runtime Kit flow from package boundary to prepared reads and named target requests.

pages.title: Package to screen
pages.description: Build the full public Runtime Kit chain from one package boundary to one React surface.
pages.summary: Walks from a package boundary through admitted material, prepared reads, target requests, and React rendering.

pages.title: Runtime Kit concept map
pages.description: How package boundaries, prepared reads, callable targets, local state, and placeable surfaces fit together.
pages.summary: Explains the full Runtime Kit concept chain with product scenes, visual flow, anti-patterns, and links to tutorial, recipe, and reference pages.

pages.title: Placeable surfaces
pages.description: Understand the generic surface descriptor shape that lets a shell place product UI without knowing product meaning.
pages.summary: Explains the placeable-surface composition primitive with visual maps: package material becomes prepared reads and targets, descriptors declare regions and allowed names, and a dumb shell/sidebar places surfaces without product-specific branches.

pages.title: Build without tangled code
pages.description: Translate normal coupled app code into Bitfield package state, prepared reads, target requests, and private UI state.
pages.summary: Shows how to translate traditional app coupling into Bitfield state, prepared inputs, target requests, private UI state, and package boundaries.

pages.title: Translate traditional code
pages.description: Turn stores, service imports, direct calls, and shell branching into Bitfield state, prepared reads, target requests, and package records.
pages.summary: Shows source-grounded translations from traditional stores, direct imports, service calls, shell branching, and package-file reads into Bitfield reads, requests, descriptors, and private UI state.

pages.title: Share state between packages
pages.description: Use Bitfield state for shared product facts instead of sharing another package's private object.
pages.summary: Explains when a value becomes shared product state and shows the selected-file shape without package-to-package store imports.

pages.title: Read data another package prepared
pages.description: Read a prepared input instead of importing another package or parsing its storage.
pages.summary: Shows how consumers read named prepared data while the preparing package keeps its files, records, and storage shape private.

pages.title: Ask another package to do work
pages.description: Send a target request instead of calling another package's implementation.
pages.summary: Shows how buttons, shortcuts, and package actions ask named targets to run work without importing implementation functions.

pages.title: Keep private UI state private
pages.description: Decide what stays in local UI state and what becomes Bitfield state.
pages.summary: Separates hover state, open menus, draft text, and drag details from shared product facts that multiple packages need.

pages.title: Store files inside a package
pages.description: Ship package-owned bytes without making consumers reach into package files.
pages.summary: Shows the package-owned file boundary and the consumer-side mistake of reading another package's files directly.

pages.title: Let many packages work together
pages.description: Combine shared state, prepared reads, target requests, and private UI state across several packages.
pages.summary: Walks a multi-package product chain where files, editors, AI context, notifications, and shells cooperate without centralizing product meaning.

pages.title: Build a file preview flow
pages.description: A source-grounded case study that translates one file, preview, help, notification, and agent flow from traditional architecture into Runtime Kit boundaries.
pages.summary: Follows a realistic file, preview, help, keyboard, notification, and private UI flow from traditional coupled code into public Runtime Kit handles.

pages.title: Rules for AI agents
pages.description: Tell AI agents how to build Bitfield features without falling back to traditional coupled app architecture.
pages.summary: Provides prompt rules, bad-output corrections, and review checks that keep generated code on public Runtime Kit boundaries.

pages.title: Read data in React
pages.description: Use the public React read hook for prepared Bitfield data.
pages.summary: Shows selectors, render states, request pairing, and what React components should not own.

pages.title: Send a request
pages.description: Send opaque payloads to Bitfield targets through the public request function.
pages.summary: Shows JSON, text, byte payloads, reply decoding, cancellation, and target-boundary mistakes.

pages.title: Packages
pages.description: Declare what a Runtime Kit package stores, ships, and makes callable.
pages.summary: Explains package sets, records, stored bytes, callable slots, validation rules, and safe package examples.

pages.title: Local state
pages.description: Understand the customer-visible Bitfield state under ~/.bitfield.
pages.summary: Explains stored data, Runtime Kit package state, activation material, and what users should not hand-edit.

pages.title: Build with AI agents
pages.description: Give an AI agent the Runtime Kit public contract so it adds features without crossing private boundaries.
pages.summary: Gives AI-agent prompt packs, bad-output corrections, review maps, and public Runtime Kit boundaries for generated feature work.

pages.title: Troubleshooting
pages.description: Find which Runtime Kit boundary broke: package admission, prepared reads, target requests, replies, local state, or activation.
pages.summary: Maps Runtime Kit symptoms to the public chain link that failed and gives safe repair checks.

pages.title: Runtime Kit Cookbook
pages.description: Complete public Runtime Kit recipes for package sets, package boundary files, prepared reads, target requests, and app-facing surfaces.
pages.summary: Indexes Runtime Kit cookbook entries for packages, package-owned bytes, callable slots, React surfaces, and placeable surface product loops.

pages.title: Package set with one record
pages.description: Create the smallest complete package boundary: one package, one record, one clear address.
pages.summary: Shows the smallest package boundary with one durable package record.

pages.title: Package-owned file
pages.description: Attach package-owned bytes to a package boundary.
pages.summary: Shows how a package declares package-owned bytes without reaching outside its folder.

pages.title: Callable package slot
pages.description: Declare a package-owned runnable door that app code can ask for work.
pages.summary: Shows how a package declares a callable slot and how app code sends a request to it.

pages.title: React surface for package data
pages.description: Read package-prepared data and send a target request from one React surface.
pages.summary: Shows the app-facing read and request shape without exposing package implementation details to React.

pages.title: Placeable surface product loop
pages.description: Build a full product loop with package records, target requests, surface descriptors, a dumb shell, and surface bodies.
pages.summary: Shows the source-owned cookbook succession for placeable surfaces: package boundary, descriptors, generic shell, surface bodies, and review checklist.

pages.title: Build with Bitfield
pages.description: The product-building map: package, surface, target, shell, device, and account loop.
pages.summary: Maps the full Bitfield build loop from product moment to package, surface, target, shell, device, and account access.

pages.title: App surfaces
pages.description: How to design a screen that reads prepared Bitfield data and sends named requests.
pages.summary: Shows how product screens read prepared data, render every state, and send named target requests.

pages.title: Package authoring
pages.description: How to shape package records, package-owned files, and callable slots for a product feature.
pages.summary: Explains how to draw a feature boundary with records, package-owned files, callable slots, expected results, and failures.

pages.title: Target design
pages.description: How to design callable targets that stay named, small, and product-shaped.
pages.summary: Gives naming, payload, reply, and failure rules for callable targets that surfaces can request safely.

pages.title: Product shells
pages.description: How shells place surfaces without owning product body logic.
pages.summary: Defines the shell boundary for navigation, layout, account/device state, and surface placement.

pages.title: Device and deployment shape
pages.description: How to think about account keys, activated devices, local state, and product rollout.
pages.summary: Connects product packages and surfaces to account keys, activated devices, local state, and rollout decisions.

pages.title: Build your own surface
pages.description: How to design another app-facing SDK without exposing Bitfield setup details.
pages.summary: Defines the public SDK boundary pattern: simple app functions outside, Bitfield setup behind the plug.

pages.title: Account and key flow
pages.description: How a customer goes from account portal to paid access, key delivery, and first device activation.
pages.summary: Explains the account portal path for creating an account, choosing access, paying securely, getting key access, and activating a device.

pages.title: Trials and billing
pages.description: How the free trial, monthly and yearly cadence, active-device ranges, and legal links fit together.
pages.summary: Explains trial billing and active-device ranges without turning pricing into hidden math.

pages.title: Active devices
pages.description: What active devices mean, how activation works, and how replacement or revocation should feel.
pages.summary: Defines active devices, local activation state, account-owned authorization, replacement, revocation, and customer-safe boundaries.

pages.title: Plan changes and cancellation
pages.description: How plan changes, cancellation, refunds, invoices, and account deletion fit into the account lifecycle.
pages.summary: Explains plan management, cancellation, invoice/refund routing, account deletion, recovery, and support links.

pages.title: How Bitfield is this fast
pages.description: A speed proof with category boundaries.
pages.summary: Explains warm local reads, memory mapping, content addresses, and non-claims.

pages.title: Warm and cold paths
pages.description: What each benchmark category includes and what it leaves out.
pages.summary: Defines warm local read, cold local read, content address lookup, durable batch write, runtime call, and network command.

pages.title: Benchmark ledger
pages.description: Public benchmark claims with category boundaries and non-claims.
pages.summary: Lists public speed claims with the work category attached.

pages.title: Measurement methodology
pages.description: How to read Bitfield benchmark numbers without comparing unlike jobs.
pages.summary: Explains warm reads, cold reads, hash lookup, durable writes, and why each number needs its category.

pages.title: Comparison guardrails
pages.description: How to compare Bitfield speed claims without mixing unlike benchmark jobs.
pages.summary: Explains which proof rows can be compared, which comparisons are category mistakes, and how to keep benchmark work shapes honest.

pages.title: Proof objections
pages.description: Straight answers to the common objections around Bitfield speed claims.
pages.summary: Answers skeptical questions about cold reads, network latency, batch ceilings, content addressing, and public disclosure boundaries.

pages.title: Runtime Kit API
pages.description: Reference for the current public Runtime Kit JavaScript values.
pages.summary: Reference for sendRequestToBitfieldTarget, useBitfieldData, payload conversion, cancellation, selectors, return states, valid examples, invalid examples, and public boundaries.

pages.title: Claim ledger
pages.description: The fields every public benchmark claim must carry.
pages.summary: Documents the public claim-ledger fields that keep speed claims honest.

pages.title: Package boundary
pages.description: Reference for things-to-store-and-run.json.
pages.summary: Lists package boundary fields, record/stored-bytes/slot thing types, payload choices, method rules, path safety, valid examples, invalid examples, and common failures.

pages.title: Changelog
pages.description: Customer-facing docs and product updates.
pages.summary: The public changelog for customer-visible docs, Runtime Kit, Build, Account, Proof, Start, and Docs changes, with links to changed pages.
